trust_action_class_identifiers.summary: Governed activation of a trust-bearing acceptance envelope or trust-policy selection.
Governed narrowing of acceptance posture, including stricter signature or root behavior.
Governed change to signer acceptance, verifier strictness, or accepted trust-level posture.
Governed declaration that a signer, artifact, trust path, or related acceptance route is revoked.
Governed definition of how revocation-bearing meaning must be carried across release-control and consumer-facing surfaces.
Publication-adjacent action whose visibility or eligibility is explicitly constrained by trust posture.
Bounded high-urgency trust action used to reduce exposure or acceptance posture under explicit review or privilege conditions.
Governed restoration or superseding trust posture that preserves prior revoked or reduced history.

revocation_continuity_class_identifiers.summary: The revocation declaration itself remains reconstructable as a typed, attributable control-plane act.
Revocation-bearing actions preserve canonical receipt continuity including gate posture and affected scope.
Historical artifacts remain recoverable without implying current trust acceptance.
Mirror visibility changes remain distinguishable from revocation truth while still preserving how visibility moved.
Consumer-facing summaries and refusal posture remain coherent enough to distinguish warned, revoked, hidden, and historical states.
Local verifier behavior and local trust-root overlays remain explainable without becoming canonical trust truth.
Any revocation shape whose declaration, receipts, archive linkage, or interpretation cannot be reconstructed canonically.

gate_receipt_archive_index_relation_note: Trust execution remains downstream of publication, trust, and licensing gate posture and does not create permission by itself.
Trust-affecting actions are operator-transaction-bearing and must emit continuity-compatible receipts.
Trust posture changes must not silently redefine release contract profile or release-index compatibility meaning.
Release-index visibility and trust acceptance remain distinct concepts.
Archive presence preserves history but does not imply current trust acceptance.
Mirror visibility preserves availability posture but does not define trust truth.
Canary and downgrade execution may be trust-gated without becoming identical to trust execution.
runtime_caution_notes: This doctrine does not prove live trust-root rotation maturity.
This doctrine does not prove live runtime cutover safety.
Any later runtime-affecting trust action remains subordinate to lifecycle, replay, snapshot, isolation, and coexistence doctrine.
Trust continuity law may reduce ambiguity for later work without authorizing hotswap or distributed authority transitions.

invalidity_failure_categories.summary: The action was attempted without the required review or privilege posture.
The requested trust posture is not canonically declared.
Signer or root behavior depends on undeclared or non-canonical root state.
The supposed revocation has no reconstructable canonical declaration.
The revocation exists in one local surface but not in the continuity-bearing record.
The effect exists only in one local verifier state with no canonical continuity record.
Publication or mirror state was treated as trust truth.
Archive or mirror state was treated as sufficient trust evidence.
Trust-bearing action history cannot be reconstructed canonically.
Trust posture was widened again without explicit continuity.
A trust-bearing claim exceeds current runtime maturity.

canonical: upstream governance, safety, runtime, and release doctrine
canonical operator transaction and receipt continuity records
canonical trust policies, governed trust-root records, and governed release-control records

prohibited_trust_revocation_shapes: mirror removal treated as revocation proof
publication visibility treated as trust truth
local verifier behavior treated as canonical trust truth
trust execution assumed safe for live runtime cutover because doctrine exists
signer or verifier posture changes recorded with no continuity-bearing receipt
